item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Dalen the Cold"
This amused me and could have made me upvote this all by itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: April civilization reminds me of Imperium of Mankind and October civilization is basically a Cult of Unseeing Eye without beholders, but "warlike tribal mathematicians" made me upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this. I'd like to find out what happens if people leave the settlement, though. Also: what happens if others are *in* the settlement when it shifts?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And they all speak English! What luck.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I find myself wishing that there was a longer testing log here.

Also, those militant tribal mathematicians, man…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The population appeared to be suffering from exhaustion at the time of manifestation.

Got a laugh out of me, and therefore an upvote. A fun read, this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I do not wish to be Dalen the Cold.

Made me chuckle

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very, very interesting concept and it is well executed. I wonder what would happen if the SCP Foundation kept a member of a civilisation outside of SCP-1607 when the transition happens.

Two tiny nitpicks:

_ I feel the containment procedures should explicitly give the measurements of the fencing (from what I understand, the SCP does have a maximum size that it never exceeds);

_ this passage is a bit awkward:

It is currently unknown if SCP-1607 is actually transporting existing settlements via what could be extra-dimensional means or simply altering itself to present the illusion of different cultures. Individuals generated by SCP-1607 claim that this first explanation is correct

it makes it look like the SCP Foundation is guess ing at trans-dimensional shenanigans with no evidence. I think it would work better if it were worded like this:

Individuals generated by SCP-1607 claim that the transitions are the result of extra-dimensional displacement. However, it is currently unknown if this explanation is correct or if SCP-1067 simply alters itself to present the illusion of different cultures.

Still, I like it a lot. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "warlike tribal mathematicians"… just… "warlike tribal mathematicians"… I though I had seen pretty much all the random in the world after reading the SCP Foundation for two years. Now I no longer think so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Haha, I love this. Can't believe I didn't know of it until now. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Anthropology! I like!

I'd like to read more about these civilisations…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The only contradiction I see is poison vs. pit-dropping.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, I've read this a few days ago, but just now had a bit of fridge logic: how does, exactly, the Dalen thing work?

successor to a deceased ruler would adopt their name and go through cosmetic surgery in order to resemble that ruler.

the twin brother of the previous ruler, who resembled them without the use of cosmetic surgery,

See, the previous ruler, he had to, at some point, succeed an even earlier ruler. And when he did so, he had to had a surgery to make him resemble him. This contradicts the statement that his twin brother resembles him without a surgery.

Actually, the way I understand it, all the Dalens look exactly the same as Dalen the First did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All you need is your trusty spoon…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A population of warlike tribal mathematicians. It considered the solving of complex equations as both a show of strength and as a proving ritual for youth. Despite their mathematical prowess, they displayed primitive levels of technology and so their initial aggression only caused negligible disruption to containment.

This here hit all my happy buttons. Upvoted with glee.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I do not wish to be Dalen the Cold.

I have too many reasons to upvote this one, but this is the funniest one XD.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How so? By supplying more detail in terms of the people's value system, language, and family structure, the people become more interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Have you read "Sideways in time"? It's extremely similar to this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You might consider a line before the culture log such as "The following is a brief summary. For more in depth information about a culture, see Document 1607-XXXX."

Also, do the gifts the Foundation make (such as the mannequin) disappear when the culture does, or does it stay behind? Similarly, what about the box of eyes-did they disappear or remain behind in Foundation hands?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Like the idea, dislike aspects of the execution. Specifically, every soceity seems to be some level of an extreme, an attempt at thinking up something strange and unique, when the majority of civilizations realistically should be fairly normal. Which, I do admit, isn't REQUIRED..in fact it's possible that that's part of the SCP object itself, that it selects the most bizarre cultures it can find. But were that to be the case I'd have expected the Foundation to make note of it, because the oddity is glaringly obvious.

For an example of my meaning, take the culture of, say, the Roman Empire, and compare it to the culture of Japan at various points in history. I'm no history buff here, so I can't give specific details. But I can say even without checking that while there's vast differences between them, no doubt, from what I do know neither were nearly so extreme in their beliefs or actions on the whole as any of the societies I see mentioned in this article. If two or three of them had been fairly bland cultures, it would have made the weirdness of the others stand out more and leave a better literary taste in my reading-mouth.

No vote, I really do like the idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A culture of warlike tribal mathematicians

And this is where I upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In the culture with blind citizens, is everyone blind? I am mainly asking this because it says they conduct surgeries to blind themselves… and I am not sure whether its possible to do a surgery while blind

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How are we able to communicate with them?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was somewhat entertained by the SCP, so I won't downvote, but I think that Sal's complaints ought to be addressed before I can upvote this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ehhhhh… It's good enough for me to upvote but I'd like to see more done with it? Do the villagers progress in between the times they are present or does it reset each time to the same events?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The October culture made me upvote. So unique…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That punchline. It made me smile.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea here, but the stuff you added directly contradicts the previous information.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is my first random SCP and I think it was decent enough to get my vote. A bit more content for each civ would be nice, perhaps some more interviews with different leaders of each culture. But as it is I think it's nice. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I haven't read the SCP yet, but just wanted to voice agreement on the phrase "1900's level of technology" being super vague. Even in the US, it wasn't until relatively recently that the tech gap between different regions became as small as it is today. You may laugh, but there were households that had TV's but still had to use a butter churn because commercial butter wasn't available to them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the Dalens, but this is just another random civilization SCP…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, Moose likes this, and he is a much more learned anthropologist than me. However, in my opinion at least, the anthroplogy in this SCP is a bit shoddy. Here's some things to think about, Tanhony:

  • The Mayans rarely, if ever, actually practiced human sacrifice. The Aztecs would be a much better comparison.
  • Culture ≠ Religion, Systems of Worship, System of Ruling, etc. - This one really bothers me. Every culture on the list looks like a summary of a nation from a Civilization. As variable as this list is in terms of religious beliefs and practices, the cultures do not seem to vary that much in their use of language, what kind of shelters they had, what they ate (considered food), what they wore, their family and clan structure, their value systems, their "foreign affairs", and their worldview in general.
  • Terms like "stone-age" would not be applicable in this situation, given that stone-age refers specifically to certain ancient areas of the so-called "old world" (Making it DOUBLE NON-APPICABLE [COULD THERE BE SUCH A THING?] since A. This is in Alaska and B. The people appearing are from entirely different universes)
  • "Culture" is kind of a funny word, and its range just doesn't quite fit with the what's appearing in this SCP. A better term might be "people with a shared cultural knowledge".

Anyways, that's my two cents. I suppose in the end this is still entertaining to most folks, mostly what ruined for me were some of the more jarring statements. For instance, "The culture showed a roughly 1900's level of technology" sounds really non-clinical, and is ambiguous (1900s level of technology where? England? Afghanistan? New Zealand? This is especially distressing if it was written by an anthropologist.) These are some cool ideas, Tanhony, but for somebody who's spent a long time reading about much stranger cultures (I recently read about how the Nuer use cow metaphors to greet, insult, compliment, and debate) this just doesn't seem that intriguing.

EDIT: Here's an anthropologized version of one of your entries, to show how much detail you can put in for variability:

August A people which purported to be highly peaceful and pacifistic. Structures and clothing were made from the byproducts of the naturally deceased, while those who did not follow the culture's laws were dropped into one of several pits located in SCP-1607. Family structure was nuclear, with retired elders committing ritualized suicide in order to continue supply of materials for structures and clothing. Plants were perceived as inedible, and diet mostly consisted of insects. Mythology told of several creator gods having created the world by creating a large framework made of bone, then wrapping human skin around the frame. Language spoken consisted mostly of guttural vowels, interspersed with clicks, however the alphabet used was of Latin origin. Cultural value appeared to lie on the human body and its usage, suggesting a historical lack of environmental resources. Causing damage to a living, human body (of oneself or another's) resulted in capital punishment and recycling of the body for building material. An unknown poison was used for execution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the fact that these places just show up from elsewhere, stay a while, and then vanish forever. I, for one, liked the interview.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this for the most part, but the interview does nothing for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I do enjoy the civilizations, I do agree that beyond their descriptions, there isn't much else to the article. I also think the interview's dialogue seems a little unnatural. Overall, though, it's still pretty interesting, so upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, well done.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This hits a lot of my happy buttons as an anthropology nerd - a bunch of broken, illogical cultures who still seem to have come from somewhere. (Hell, this gives me Library ideas…) I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm always a bit iffy when it comes to random generator SCPs. This is well written, but it feels very barebones compared to the other random generators we have.
